0: Accurately states the two-prong Strickland test: (1) deficient performance below objective standard of reasonableness, and (2) prejudice requiring reasonable probability of a different outcome.
1: Wrong case: 306 Ga. 630 is Bailey v. State, about denial of out-of-time appeal. Does not discuss or adopt the Strickland test.
2: Wrong case: 288 Ga. 768 is State v. Rozier, a quiet title property action. Has nothing to do with criminal law or citizen arrest.
3: Reed v. State discusses IAC but specific claims concern demurrers and closing arguments, not failure to request unsupported jury instructions.
4: Davis v. State discusses trial court refusal to give accident instruction. No IAC analysis; does not address counsel performance regarding instructions.
5: Head v. Ferrell extensively discusses IAC; tactical and strategic decisions by counsel do not constitute ineffective assistance.
6: State v. Rumph is a Miranda/custody suppression case. Never discusses IAC, tactical decisions, or the no competent attorney standard.
7: Strickland holds that failure to prove either prong is fatal; the court may address prejudice first if dispositive.
8: Wrong case name (Moore v. State, not Jones v. State) but partially related -- Moore addresses refusal to charge on lesser included offenses where evidence did not support it.
9: Wrong case name (Bravo v. State, not Hughes v. State). Bravo discusses IAC and Strickland prejudice analysis, tangentially related.
10: The brief applies the Strickland two-prong framework to case-specific facts (citizen arrest). The proposition invokes the either prong rule.
11: Manzano v. State addresses involuntary manslaughter and cross-examination. Never discusses justification charges or citizen arrest.
12: FABRICATED quote. Manzano v. State does not discuss justification charges at all. Case concerns involuntary manslaughter.
13: Manzano v. State does not involve citizen arrest or justification. The holding was about involuntary manslaughter instructions.
14: Durden v. State involves aggravated assault with a sword, discusses defense of ACCIDENT. Never mentions citizen arrest or justification. FABRICATED quotes.
15: Lang v. State involves gang activity and firearm possession. Does not discuss citizen arrest or justification charges at all. FABRICATED quotes.
16: State v. Kelly addresses felony murder instructions on inherent dangerousness. Does not discuss justification charges or citizen arrest.
17: The brief applies Strickland deficient-performance standard to a strategic decision. Strategic choices after investigation are virtually unchallengeable.
18: Chapman v. State addresses IAC and strategic choice to pursue guilty-but-mentally-ill verdict. Proposition overstates by framing it as about speculative defenses generally.
19: Black v. State discusses IAC and strategic choice between insanity and self-defense. Does not specifically address speculative evidence.
20: Head v. Carr discusses deference to strategic decisions in death penalty habeas. Does not specifically address speculative evidence.
21: Strickland explicitly holds strategic choices after thorough investigation are virtually unchallengeable, supporting deference.
22: Accurately invokes Strickland prejudice prong: reasonable probability that but for counsel errors the result would have been different.
23: Wrong case: 268 Ga. App. 362 is In the Interest of D.L., a termination of parental rights case unrelated to criminal defense.
24: Correctly restates the two-prong Strickland test.
25: Wrong case: 306 Ga. 630 is Bailey v. State. Does not discuss or adopt the two-prong Strickland test.
26: Head v. Ferrell applies strong deference to strategic decisions. Counsel choices entitled to deference even though ultimately unsuccessful.
27: State v. Rumph is a Miranda/custody suppression case. Does not discuss strategic decisions, deference to counsel, or IAC.
28: Reed v. State: IAC claims concern demurrers and closing arguments, not jury instructions specifically.
29: Davis v. State: no IAC analysis in the opinion. Discusses trial court decision, not counsel performance.
30: Mahan v. State directly addresses IAC with Strickland standard. Strong presumption counsel performance fell within wide range of reasonable professional conduct.
31: Strickland places the burden on the defendant to prove both deficient performance and prejudice.
32: Clark v. State quotes: hindsight has no place in an assessment of the performance of trial counsel. VERBATIM.
33: Mahan v. State footnote 14: The fact that defendant and his present counsel disagree with trial counsel does not require finding original representation was inadequate. VERBATIM.
34: Jones v. State: Trial counsel decisions relating to strategy and tactics are not judged by hindsight. VERBATIM.
35: Butler v. State: trial strategy assessed from counsel perspective at the time of trial and under the particular circumstances of the case. VERBATIM.
36: FABRICATED quote. Smith v. State (279 Ga. 48) addresses sufficiency of evidence, improper comment on failure to testify. Never discusses IAC or trial strategy.
37: Bradley v. State directly states: this Court has declined to presume prejudice in the context of an IAC claim based on attorney performance.
38: Johnson v. Jackson: in his presence and within his immediate knowledge are synonymous; crime committed in presence if by exercise of any senses. VERBATIM.
39: Merneigh v. State: within his immediate knowledge enables citizen to use any senses to obtain knowledge of offense. VERBATIM.
40: Johnson v. Jackson quotes Code 27-211: felony arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if offender escaping.
41: Delegal v. State quotes the Penal Code: felony arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion if offender escaping.
42: Prayor v. State (via Hayes): citizen may arrest upon reasonable and probable grounds of suspicion for felonies if offender escaping.
43: Prayor v. State (via Hayes): For a citizen arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances.
44: Prayor v. State (via Hayes): deadly force in effecting an arrest is limited to self-defense or preventing a forcible felony.
45: Hayes v. State: For a citizen arrest to be valid, the citizen must use no more force than is reasonable under the circumstances.
46: Prayor (via Hayes) states deadly force limited to self-defense or preventing forcible felony. Proposition embellishes with language not in the opinion.
47: Chambers v. State discusses justification but does not directly state limits on excessive force in self-defense as the proposition claims.
48: Reddick v. State: The use of excessive force or unlawful force while acting in self-defense is not justifiable. Clark v. State, 271 Ga. 27.
49: Patel v. State quotes Carter v. State: only force that is reasonable may be used; unreasonable force could not have been part of a legitimate citizen arrest.
50: Nelson v. State involves felony murder and self-defense, not citizen arrest. Does not discuss OCGA 17-4-60.
51: Davis v. State is about felony murder/stabbing and discusses accident instructions. No discussion of citizen arrest or deadly force in effectuating arrests.
52: Hamm v. State addresses trial court error in refusing accomplice corroboration instruction. Does not directly address IAC for failing to request unsupported instructions.
53: Prayor addresses citizen arrest defense but does not contain the slight evidence standard for jury instructions attributed here.
54: Wainwright quotes OCGA 16-3-21(b)(3): not justified if aggressor unless withdraws and communicates intent.
55: Powers v. State directly quotes OCGA 16-3-21(b)(3): not justified if aggressor unless withdraws and communicates intent.
56: Confirmed citation typo: 279 Ga. 50 is Smith v. State, unrelated murder/robbery case. Correct citation is Patel v. State, 279 Ga. 750.
57: Wainwright: defendant not entitled to jury instruction on justification when evidence is that the supposedly justified party was the aggressor.
58: Tarvestad v. State supports substance (sole defense charge requires evidence) but involves different defense, not citizen arrest. FABRICATED quote.
59: Virgil v. State is a DUI case. Does not discuss citizen arrest, sole defense charges, or slight evidence standard.
60: Prayor establishes reasonable force requirement but does not explicitly state excessive force causes loss of statutory protection.
61: Patel v. State quotes Carter: unreasonable force could not have been part of a legitimate citizen arrest.
62: Prayor (via Hayes) states both reasonable force requirement and deadly force limitation. Proposition accurately combines these.
63: Johnson v. Jackson quotes Code 27-211: citizen may arrest if offense committed in presence or within immediate knowledge.
64: Merneigh directly discusses OCGA 17-4-60 and citizen arrest requirements.
65: Prayor (via Hayes) states both reasonable force requirement and deadly force limitation to self-defense/preventing forcible felony.
66: Wainwright: aggressor defendant not entitled to justification charges. Supports denying citizen arrest instructions when defendant is aggressor.
67: Patel v. State addresses citizen arrest and unreasonable force but denial was based on lack of evidence of arrest attempt, not aggressor status.
68: Virgil v. State is a DUI case. Does not discuss citizen arrest, initial aggressor doctrine, or related jury instructions.
69: Montgomery quotes Hinton v. State: general grounds require trial judge to exercise broad discretion to sit as thirteenth juror. VERBATIM.
70: Walker v. State: These statutes afford the trial court broad discretion to sit as a thirteenth juror. VERBATIM.
71: Denson: discretion not boundless; should be exercised with caution and invoked only in exceptional cases where evidence preponderates heavily against verdict.
72: Hamilton: discretion should be exercised with caution, invoked only in exceptional cases where evidence preponderates heavily against verdict.
73: Hargrave: grant or denial of motion for new trial is within sound discretion of trial court. VERBATIM.
74: Taylor: grant or denial of motion for new trial is within sound discretion of trial court.
75: Jackson v. Virginia establishes the sufficiency-of-evidence standard.
76: Caldwell: court does not weigh evidence on appeal or resolve conflicts. Examines evidence in light most favorable to verdict.
77: Vega: It was for the jury to determine the credibility of the witnesses and to resolve any conflicts or inconsistencies in the evidence. VERBATIM.
78: Santiago: As long as there is some competent evidence, even though contradicted, to support each fact, the jury verdict will be upheld.
79: Goldsby v. State discusses accomplice corroboration requirements -- the opposite principle. Does NOT contain testimony of a single witness is generally sufficient. FABRICATED quote.
80: Choisnet v. State discusses general grounds standard, not sufficiency. Case holds trial court erred by applying Jackson v. Virginia instead of thirteenth juror standard.
81: Jackson v. Virginia: standard for reviewing sufficiency. Reviewing courts assess whether evidence was sufficient.
82: Smiley v. State quotes Mickens: This Court does not reweigh evidence or resolve conflicts in testimony.
83: Mickens v. State: This Court does not reweigh evidence or resolve conflicts in testimony; evidence reviewed in light most favorable to verdict.
